33	# Effort Breaker
34	
35	You take **one high-effort blueprint declaration** and split it into a `\uses{}`-linked chain of **smaller, easier sub-lemmas**, so the prover formalizes a sequence of small goals instead of one large one. The target's proof becomes "by `L1`, `L2`, …, `L_n`", each `L_i` is a new block with its own statement, informal proof, and `\uses{}`, and each `L_i` is strictly easier than the original.
36	
37	You decompose DOWN (one node → many smaller ones). This is the complement of the dag-walker (which fills the cone UP). You do not invent new mathematics — you re-express the target's existing proof as a chain of named intermediate results.

69	1. Read the target block on disk and inspect it: `archon dag-query node --node <target> --json` (effort, deps, used-by). `archon` is on PATH; `--json` is parseable (banner on stderr).

71	3. For each step, author a new sub-lemma block in the appropriate chapter:
72	   - a `\label{lem:<descriptive>}` and a `\lean{...}` hint (the Lean name the prover will use; name it by convention if the directive doesn't give one, and note it for the plan agent),

It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	Archon talks back to the user through **`TO_USER.md`** — a single, *persistent* notice board surfaced as a UI banner. It is **shared and writable by every agent** (plan, prover, review). It is no longer cleared at the start of each review phase; content survives across iters until an agent prunes it. The discipline every writer follows:
112	
113	- **Concise**: the whole file stays ≤ 2–3 short bullets. It is a banner, not a log.
114	- **Relevant now**: before adding anything, read the current content and **delete any bullet that is no longer true at this point** (the blocker was resolved, the decision was overtaken, the credential got set). Never let it accumulate stale notices.
115	- **Notice board, never a question queue**: the loop is autonomous and may run unattended for many iters. Never write "awaiting your decision", an options menu, a "where to reply", or "default to X if no reply" — anything implying the loop is paused for a human. State decisions as *made* and blockers as *worked-around-as-far-as-possible*; the user steers asynchronously via `USER_HINTS.md`.
116	
117	## Agent Roles
118	
119	### Plan Agent
120	
121	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
122	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
123	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
124	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
125	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
126	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
127	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
128	
129	### Prover Agent
130	
131	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
132	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
133	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
134	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
135	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
136	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
137	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
138	
139	### Subagents
140	
141	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
142	
143	### Review Agent
144	
145	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
146	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
147	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
148	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
149	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
150	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
151	
152	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
153	
154	These run automatically between phases:

156	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
157	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
158	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.

5	% STRATEGY NOTE. This chapter packages the classical Grothendieck/Nitsure~\S4
6	% \emph{generic flatness} theorem: over a noetherian integral base, a coherent
7	% sheaf on a finite-type \(X \to S\) becomes flat over a non-empty open
8	% subscheme of \(S\). Both its algebraic form (generic freeness of a finite
9	% module over a finite-type algebra over a noetherian domain) and its
10	% geometric form are recorded here as the prover-ready specification for
11	% \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FlatteningStratification.lean}.

16	\section{Setup and motivation}
17	\label{sec:flatstrat_setup}
18	
19	Generic flatness is the foundational input to the flattening-stratification
20	construction underlying Grothendieck's existence theorem for the Quot scheme
21	(\cite{nitsure-hilbert-quot}, \S4--\S5).  Its content is that flatness of a
22	coherent sheaf is a generic property over an integral base: over a noetherian
23	integral scheme \(S\), a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module on a finite-type morphism
24	\(X \to S\) becomes flat after restricting to a non-empty open subscheme of
25	\(S\). Algebraically this is the statement that a finite module over a
26	finite-type algebra over a noetherian domain becomes free after inverting a
27	single non-zero element of the base ring.
28	
29	We work throughout with a noetherian (integral, where required) base scheme
30	\(S\), a finite-type morphism \(p : X \to S\), and a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module
31	\(\F\).
32	
33	\section{Generic flatness}
34	\label{sec:generic_flatness}
35	
36	The construction of the flattening stratification proceeds by Noetherian
37	induction on \(S\). The induction step relies on the following classical
38	\emph{generic flatness} theorem of Grothendieck: over a noetherian integral
39	base, a coherent sheaf is flat over a non-empty open subscheme. Algebraically,
40	this is the assertion that a finitely generated module over a finite-type
41	algebra over a noetherian domain is generically free after inverting a single
42	non-zero element of the base ring.

218	  closed under short exact sequences (\(P(N_1)\) and \(P(N_3)\) imply \(P(N_2)\)
219	  for \(0 \to N_1 \to N_2 \to N_3 \to 0\)). Then \(P\) holds for every finite
220	  \(B\)-module. In particular every finite \(B\)-module \(M\) admits a finite
221	  filtration \(0 = M_0 \subset \cdots \subset M_r = M\) with each quotient

223	  prove generic freeness for \(M\) it suffices to prove it for each
224	  \(B/\mathfrak{p}\) and to splice along the filtration's short exact sequences.

254	  \(N_f\) is free over \(A_f\).

260	  localisation away from \(f'\): the ring \(A_f\) is the localisation of \(A_{f'}\)

262	  \(N_{f'}\) at the image of \(f''\). A localisation of a free module is free over

264	  \(N_f\) over \(A_f\). Hence \(N_f\) is free over \(A_f\). Stated as a standalone
265	  fact, it is applied to both ends of the spliced sequence.

473	  % IsIntegral.exists_multiple_integral_of_isLocalization applied to the generator AS A

500	  lemma \cref{lem:gf_clear_one_denominator} provides the same denominator-clearing primitive

503	  internally.)

506	  is the \(A_g\)-algebra map \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \to B_g\) sending \(X_j \mapsto b_j\),

508	  module-finiteness through a comparison with the situation over \(K\).

510	  \emph{Step 3a — the comparison map \(\nu : B_g \to B_K\).} Since \(g \ne 0\) in the
511	  domain \(A\) and \(A \subseteq K\), the image of \(g\) is a unit of the field \(K\), so
512	  it is invertible in \(B_K = K \otimes_A B\). Hence the canonical \(A\)-algebra map
513	  \(B \to B_K\), \(b \mapsto 1 \otimes b\), sends \(g\) to a unit and, by the universal
514	  property of localisation, factors through a canonical \(A\)-algebra map
515	  \[
516	    \nu : B_g = B[g^{-1}] \longrightarrow B_K, \qquad \nu(b/g^k) = (1 \otimes b)\,(1 \otimes g)^{-k}.

518	  The map \(\nu\) is injective. Indeed, \(B\) is a domain, and both \(B_g\) and \(B_K\) are
519	  localisations of \(B\) at multiplicative subsets of the non-zerodivisors: \(B_g\) inverts
520	  the powers of \(g\), while \(B_K = K \otimes_A B\) inverts (in particular) the images of

525	  domain over which \(A\) acts faithfully is \(0\)), so \(\nu(b/g^k) = 0\) forces
526	  \(1 \otimes b = 0\), hence \(b = 0\) and \(b/g^k = 0\). Under \(\nu\) the lifts

528	  Step 1: \(\nu(b_j) = \bar b_j\).

531	  \(K \to A_g\).} Injectivity of the \(A_g\)-algebra map
532	  \(\varphi : A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \to B_g\), \(X_j \mapsto b_j\), is equivalent to the
533	  algebraic independence of the family \((b_j)\) over \(A_g\): a non-zero element of the
534	  kernel is exactly a non-trivial \(A_g\)-polynomial relation \(P(b_1, \dots, b_n) = 0\).
535	  The localisation \(A_g = A[g^{-1}] \subseteq K\) embeds in the fraction field, giving an
536	  injection of \(A_g\)-algebras \(A_g \hookrightarrow K\). Now suppose
537	  \(P \in A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) is non-zero with \(P(b_1, \dots, b_n) = 0\) in \(B_g\).
538	  Applying the injective map \(\nu\) and using \(\nu(b_j) = \bar b_j\) together with the fact
539	  that \(\nu\) is an \(A\)-algebra map extending scalars to \(K\), we obtain
540	  \(P^K(\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n) = 0\) in \(B_K\), where \(P^K\) is the image of \(P\)
541	  under the coefficient inclusion \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \hookrightarrow K[X_1, \dots, X_n]\).
542	  Since \(A_g \hookrightarrow K\) is injective, \(P \ne 0\) gives \(P^K \ne 0\); but the
543	  \(\bar b_j\) are algebraically independent over \(K\) (Step 1), so the only \(K\)-polynomial
544	  vanishing on them is \(0\), a contradiction. Hence no non-trivial relation exists, \((b_j)\)
545	  is algebraically independent over \(A_g\), and \(\varphi\) is injective.
546	
547	  \emph{Step 3c — module-finiteness of \(B_g\) over \(\operatorname{im}\varphi\).} By Step 1,
548	  \(B_K\) is module-finite over \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\); fix a finite set of algebra
549	  generators of \(B_K\) over \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\) (Step 2). Each such generator
550	  satisfies a monic equation of integral dependence whose coefficients are polynomials in
551	  \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\). The element \(g\) of Step 2 was chosen as a common
552	  denominator for the (finitely many) coefficient polynomials of these equations, so that
553	  after inverting \(g\) every such coefficient already lies in
554	  \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n] = \operatorname{im}\varphi\). Each chosen generator is the
555	  \(\nu\)-image of an element of \(B_g\), and its integral-dependence equation is the
556	  \(\nu\)-image of the corresponding monic equation holding in \(B_g\) with coefficients in
557	  \(\operatorname{im}\varphi\); since \(\nu\) is injective, that equation holds in \(B_g\)
558	  itself. Thus each generator of \(B_g\) is integral over the subalgebra
559	  \(\operatorname{im}\varphi\), and these generators generate \(B_g\) as an
560	  \(\operatorname{im}\varphi\)-algebra. A finite-type algebra that is integral over a
561	  subalgebra is module-finite over it; hence \(B_g\) is module-finite over
562	  \(\operatorname{im}\varphi = A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\). Transported along the injection
563	  \(\varphi\), this is exactly module-finiteness of \(B_g\) over \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) for
564	  the algebra structure that \(\varphi\) puts on \(B_g\), as claimed. This recovers the
565	  source's conclusion that \(B_g\) is finite over \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\).
566	\end{proof}

569	[injectivity of a localisation lift]
570	  \label{lem:gf_isLocalization_lift_injective}
571	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.isLocalization_lift_injective}
572	  Let \(S\) be a localisation of a ring \(R\) at a multiplicative set \(M\), and let
573	  \(g : R \to P\) be a ring homomorphism sending every element of \(M\) to a unit of
574	  \(P\), so that it induces the lift \(\widehat g : S \to P\). If both the localisation
575	  map \(R \to S\) and \(g\) itself are injective, then \(\widehat g\) is injective.
576	\end{lemma}
577	\begin{proof}
578	  The lift is characterised by \(\widehat g(r/m) = g(r)\,g(m)^{-1}\). By the universal
579	  criterion for injectivity of a localisation lift, \(\widehat g\) is injective iff for
580	  all \(r_1, r_2 \in R\) the equality of localisation images
581	  \((r_1 \mapsto S) = (r_2 \mapsto S)\) implies \(g(r_1) = g(r_2)\). Both the hypothesis
582	  and the conclusion of that implication reduce, via injectivity of \(R \to S\) and of
583	  \(g\), to the single condition \(r_1 = r_2\); hence the implication holds and
584	  \(\widehat g\) is injective. (This helper is applied twice in
585	  \cref{lem:gf_noether_clear_denominators}, to the comparison maps \(\nu : B_g \to B_K\)
586	  and \(\psi : A_g \to K\).)
587	\end{proof}

620	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(d \ge 0\), and \(N\) a finite module over the
621	  polynomial ring \(P_d := A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) (with its induced \(A\)-module structure
622	  through the scalar tower \(A \to P_d \to N\)). Then there exist \(m \in \mathbb{N}\) and

624	  \(T := N / \operatorname{im}\varphi\) is a torsion \(P_d\)-module. Equivalently, there is
625	  a short exact sequence of \(P_d\)-modules
626	  \[
627	    0 \to P_d^{\oplus m} \xrightarrow{\ \varphi\ } N \to T \to 0,
628	    \qquad T \text{ torsion}.
629	  \]
630	
631	  The integer \(m\) is the \emph{generic rank} of \(N\): the dimension of the
632	  \(\mathrm{Frac}(P_d)\)-vector space
633	  \(\mathrm{Frac}(P_d) \otimes_{P_d} N\), i.e.\ of the localisation of \(N\) at the
634	  non-zero-divisors of the domain \(P_d\). This step lives over \(P_d\) directly —
635	  \emph{no inversion of any element \(g \in A\) is required here}; the element \(g\) of
636	  Nitsure's argument enters only at the reindexing step \cref{lem:gf_torsion_reindex}.

639	\begin{proof}
640	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1760-L1765).
641	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (generic-rank SES).}

643	  (Hilbert basis theorem, and a polynomial ring over a domain is a domain). Let
644	  \(Q = \mathrm{Frac}(P_d)\) be its fraction field and
645	  \(N_Q := Q \otimes_{P_d} N\) the localisation of \(N\) at the non-zero-divisors of
646	  \(P_d\); as \(N\) is finite over \(P_d\), \(N_Q\) is a finite-dimensional
647	  \(Q\)-vector space. Put \(m := \dim_Q N_Q\), the generic rank.
648	
649	  Choose a \(Q\)-basis of \(N_Q\) and clear denominators: each basis vector is a
650	  \(Q\)-unit multiple of the image of some \(v_i \in N\), so the images of
651	  \(v_1, \dots, v_m \in N\) again form a \(Q\)-basis of \(N_Q\). In particular the family
652	  \((v_i)_{i}\) is linearly independent over \(Q\); restricting scalars along the
653	  injection \(P_d \hookrightarrow Q\), it is linearly independent over \(P_d\). Let
654	  \(\varphi : P_d^{\oplus m} \to N\) be the \(P_d\)-linear map
655	  \((c_1, \dots, c_m) \mapsto \sum_i c_i v_i\). Linear independence of \((v_i)\) over
656	  \(P_d\) makes \(\varphi\) injective.
657	
658	  Set \(T := N / \operatorname{im}\varphi\). Because the images of \(v_1, \dots, v_m\)
659	  already span \(N_Q\) over \(Q\), the localised map \(\varphi \otimes Q : Q^{\oplus m}
660	  \to N_Q\) is an isomorphism, so \(Q \otimes_{P_d} T = 0\). A finite \(P_d\)-module whose
661	  localisation at the non-zero-divisors of \(P_d\) vanishes is torsion; hence \(T\) is a
662	  torsion \(P_d\)-module. This is the asserted short exact sequence.
663	\end{proof}

1500	\subsection{Geometric bridges for the affine assembly}
1501	\label{sec:gf_geometric_bridges}
1502	
1503	The geometric form of generic flatness is assembled from the algebraic core by
1504	restricting to a non-empty affine open of \(S\) and working patchwise over a finite
1505	affine cover of the preimage. Two genuinely geometric steps are isolated here as
1506	named bridges: the affine-local finiteness of the section module of a finite-type
1507	quasi-coherent sheaf (G1), feeding the algebraic core its finite-module input; and
1508	the flat-locality assembly (G3), promoting per-patch freeness to flatness of the
1509	restricted sheaf over the base open. Neither is supplied directly by Mathlib: Mathlib
1510	does not provide the affine-local identification of \(\F|_W\) with the associated sheaf
1511	of \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) together with the finiteness this transports.
1512	
1513	\begin{lemma}[G1 — finite-type quasi-coherent sheaf has finite affine sections]
1514	  \label{lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections}
1515	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections}
1516	  \uses{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}
1517	  % LEAN STATUS: G1 — project-built (mathlib-build target this iter); Mathlib has only
1518	  % the abstract SheafOfModules.IsFiniteType / IsQuasicoherent local-generator
1519	  % predicates, not this affine-local finiteness.
1520	  % NOTE: overlaps the QUOT keystone lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen — to be
1521	  % extracted to shared qcoh-affine-local infra (see STRATEGY Open questions).
1522	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB
1523	  % (Lemma "Characterizing modules of finite type", \S "Characterizing modules of
1524	  % finite type and finite presentation")
1525	  % (read from references/stacks-properties.tex, L2092-L2098).
1526	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1527	  % "Let $X = \Spec(R)$ be an affine scheme.
1528	  %  The quasi-coherent sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules
1529	  %  $\widetilde M$ is a finite type $\mathcal{O}_X$-module
1530	  %  if and only if $M$ is a finite $R$-module."
1531	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB.}
1532	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\F : X.\mathrm{Modules}\) a quasi-coherent sheaf of
1533	  modules with \(\F\) of finite type. Then for every affine open \(W \subseteq X\), the
1534	  section module \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) is a finite module over \(\Gamma(X, W)\).
1535	\end{lemma}
1536	
1537	\begin{proof}
1538	  \uses{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}
1539	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (Tag 01PB, L2092-L2098;
1540	  % the Stacks proof reduces finite-type on a standard open cover to the algebra
1541	  % gluing lemma for finite generation of $M$ from finite generation of each $M_{f_i}$).
1542	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB.}
1543	  Fix an affine open \(W \subseteq X\) and write \(R = \Gamma(X, W)\) and
1544	  \(N = \Gamma(\F, W)\). Identifying \(W\) with \(\Spec R\), quasi-coherence of \(\F\)
1545	  presents \(\F|_W\) as the associated sheaf \(\widetilde N\) of the \(R\)-module \(N\):
1546	  on a basic open \(D(g) \subseteq W\) the sections are the localized module
1547	  \(\Gamma(\F, D(g)) = N_g\) over \(\Gamma(X, D(g)) = R_g\)
1548	  (\cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}). The finite-type hypothesis on
1549	  \(\F\) says \(\F\) is locally generated by finitely many sections; restricted to the
1550	  quasi-compact affine \(W\), this yields a standard open cover
1551	  \(W = \bigcup_{i=1}^{n} D(g_i)\) on each member of which \(\widetilde N|_{D(g_i)}\) is
1552	  generated by finitely many sections, i.e.\ each localization \(N_{g_i}\) is a finite
1553	  \(R_{g_i}\)-module. The elements \(g_1, \dots, g_n\) generate the unit ideal of \(R\)
1554	  (the \(D(g_i)\) cover \(\Spec R\)); by the standard finite-generation gluing
1555	  criterion — finite generation of \(N\) over \(R\) is local on such a cover — the
1556	  module \(N\) is finite over \(R\). Hence \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) is a finite
1557	  \(\Gamma(X, W)\)-module.
1558	\end{proof}
1559	
1560	\begin{lemma}[G3 — flat-locality assembly from per-patch freeness]
1561	  \label{lem:gf_flat_locality_assembly}
1562	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_flat_locality_assembly}
1563	  \uses{lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections}
1564	  % LEAN STATUS: G3 — project-built; the geometric glue identifying Γ(F, W∩W_j)
1565	  % localisations across the source cover and the base-restriction is the missing
1566	  % plumbing. Stub-level this iter; built after G1.
1567	  Let \(p : X \to S\) be a finite-type morphism with \(S\) integral and locally
1568	  noetherian, and \(\F\) a finite-type quasi-coherent \(\OO_X\)-module. Fix a non-empty
1569	  affine open \(U_0 = \Spec A \subseteq S\) with \(A\) a noetherian domain, a finite
1570	  affine cover \(\{W_j = \Spec B_j\}_j\) of \(p^{-1}(U_0)\), and an element
1571	  \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that for every \(j\) the localized section module
1572	  \((M_j)_f\) (with \(M_j = \Gamma(\F, W_j)\)) is free over \(A_f\). Set
1573	  \(V = D(f) \subseteq U_0\). Then for every affine open \(U \le V\) of \(S\) and every
1574	  affine open \(W \le p^{-1}(U)\) of \(X\), the section module \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) is flat
1575	  over \(\Gamma(S, U)\).
1576	\end{lemma}
1577	
1578	\begin{proof}
1579	  \uses{lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections}
1580	  Free modules are flat (\(\OO\)-flatness of a free module), so each \((M_j)_f\) is flat
1581	  over \(A_f\). Flatness of a module is local on the source: a module is flat over a
1582	  ring iff it is flat after localizing at every maximal ideal, and the family
1583	  \(\{W_j\}\) covers \(p^{-1}(U_0)\), so the per-patch flatness statements assemble to
1584	  flatness of the section module of \(\F\) over \(A_f = \Gamma(S, V)\) on the whole
1585	  preimage \(p^{-1}(V)\). The flat locus is moreover stable under base-restriction along
1586	  the open immersion \(U \hookrightarrow V\): for an affine \(U \le V\) the ring
1587	  \(\Gamma(S, U)\) is a localization of \(\Gamma(S, V) = A_f\) (an open immersion of
1588	  affines into a basic open being a localization), and for an affine \(W \le p^{-1}(U)\)
1589	  the module \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) is the corresponding localization of the section module
1590	  over the relevant patch \(W_j\) (via \cref{lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections}
1591	  identifying the finite section modules and their localizations across the cover).
1592	  Flatness is preserved under localization of both the ring and the module, so
1593	  \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) is flat over \(\Gamma(S, U)\), as claimed.
1594	\end{proof}

1645	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``generic flatness''.}
1646	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian integral scheme, \(p : X \to S\) a finite-type morphism,
1647	  and \(\F\) a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module. Then there exists a non-empty open
1648	  subscheme \(U \subseteq S\) such that the restriction of \(\F\) to
1649	  \(X_U = p^{-1}(U)\) is \(\OO_U\)-flat.
1650	
1651	  The coherence hypothesis on \(\F\) is essential: a non-coherent \(\OO_X\)-module
1652	  need not become flat over any non-empty open of \(S\).
1653	\end{theorem}
1654	
1655	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "The above theorem has the following consequence, which
1656	% follows by restricting attention to a non-empty affine open subscheme of \(S\)."
1657	% (Source: nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1776-L1777. The
1658	% Nitsure proof is the single sentence quoted: deduce the geometric form from
1659	% the algebraic generic flatness statement (\cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic})
1660	% by restricting to a non-empty
1661	% affine open of \(S\), where the situation reduces to the algebraic statement.)
1662	\begin{proof}
1663	  \uses{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic, lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections,
1664	        lem:gf_flat_locality_assembly}
1665	  Restrict to a non-empty affine open \(U_0 = \Spec A \subseteq S\), where \(A\) is a
1666	  noetherian domain (\(S\) integral and locally noetherian). We assemble the witness
1667	  \(V\) from the algebraic form on a finite affine cover.
1668	
1669	  \emph{Step 1 — finite affine cover above \(U_0\).} Since \(p\) is quasi-compact (by
1670	  the \([\mathrm{QuasiCompact}\,p]\) hypothesis) and \(U_0\) is affine hence
1671	  quasi-compact, the preimage \(X_{U_0} = p^{-1}(U_0)\) is quasi-compact; choose a
1672	  finite affine cover \(\{W_j = \Spec B_j\}_{j}\) of \(X_{U_0}\) by affine opens of
1673	  \(X\). Because \(p\) is locally of finite type, each ring \(B_j = \Gamma(X, W_j)\)
1674	  is a finite-type \(A\)-algebra.
1675	
1676	  \emph{Step 2 — read off a finite module on each patch.} On each \(W_j\), quasi-coherence
1677	  of \(\F\) identifies \(\F|_{W_j}\) with the associated sheaf \(\widetilde{M_j}\) of the
1678	  section module \(M_j = \Gamma(\F, W_j)\), and the finite-section bridge
1679	  \cref{lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections} — applied to the affine open \(W_j\) — makes
1680	  \(M_j\) a finite \(B_j\)-module. Through the scalar tower \(A \to B_j \to M_j\),
1681	  \(M_j\) is the data fed to the algebraic form.
1682	
1683	  \emph{Step 3 — apply the algebraic form and take the common basic open.}
1684	  \cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic} applied to \((A, B_j, M_j)\) yields \(f_j \in A\),
1685	  \(f_j \ne 0\), with \((M_j)_{f_j}\) free over \(A_{f_j}\). As the cover is finite, set
1686	  \(f := \prod_j f_j \ne 0\) (a non-zero element of the domain \(A\)) and
1687	  \(V := D(f) \subseteq \Spec A \subseteq S\), a non-empty basic open; over \(V\) every
1688	  \((M_j)_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
1689	
1690	  \emph{Step 4 — freeness implies flatness.} On each patch the localized section module
1691	  is free, hence flat over \(A_f\); the flat-locality assembly
1692	  \cref{lem:gf_flat_locality_assembly} promotes these per-patch freeness statements to
1693	  the fibrewise flatness conclusion, via the criterion that a module flat at every
1694	  maximal ideal is flat together with the locality of flatness on the source cover. This
1695	  produces the required \(V\) with \(\F|_{X_V}\) flat over \(\OO_V\).
1696	\end{proof}

2075	\section{Characterizing modules of finite type and finite presentation}
2076	\label{section-characterizing-finite-type-presentation}
2077	
2078	\noindent
2079	Let $X$ be a scheme.
2080	Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.
2081	The following lemma implies that $\mathcal{F}$ is of finite type
2082	(see Modules, Definition \ref{modules-definition-finite-type})
2083	if and only if $\mathcal{F}$ is
2084	on each open affine $\Spec(A) = U \subset X$
2085	of the form $\widetilde M$ for some finite $A$-module $M$.
2086	Similarly, $\mathcal{F}$ is of finite presentation
2087	(see Modules, Definition \ref{modules-definition-finite-presentation})
2088	if and only if $\mathcal{F}$ is
2089	on each open affine $\Spec(A) = U \subset X$
2090	of the form $\widetilde M$ for some finitely presented $A$-module $M$.
2091	
2092	\begin{lemma}
2093	\label{lemma-finite-type-module}
2094	Let $X = \Spec(R)$ be an affine scheme.
2095	The quasi-coherent sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules
2096	$\widetilde M$ is a finite type $\mathcal{O}_X$-module
2097	if and only if $M$ is a finite $R$-module.
2098	\end{lemma}
2099	
2100	\begin{proof}
2101	Assume $\widetilde M$ is a finite type $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.
2102	This means there exists an open covering of $X$ such that
2103	$\widetilde M$ restricted to the members of this covering is
2104	globally generated by finitely many sections.
2105	Thus there also exists a standard open covering
2106	$X = \bigcup_{i = 1, \ldots, n} D(f_i)$ such that $\widetilde M|_{D(f_i)}$
2107	is generated by finitely many sections. Thus $M_{f_i}$ is finitely
2108	generated for each $i$. Hence we conclude by
2109	Algebra, Lemma \ref{algebra-lemma-cover}.
2110	\end{proof}
2111

2113	\label{lemma-finite-presentation-module}

% ============================================================================
% G1 decomposition (Stacks 01PB). The affine-local finiteness of the section
% module splits into TWO halves:
%   Half 1 — LOCALITY reduction (already proved in Lean): if the section module
%     is finite on each member of a finite basic-open cover, it is finite on the
%     affine. Blocks: lem:finite_localizedModule_transfer,
%     lem:gf_finite_sections_of_basicOpen_finite_cover.
%   Half 2 — finite-type BASE CASE: on a globally-generated affine the section
%     module is finite, via Gamma-exactness on affine quasi-coherent modules.
%     Blocks (seams): lem:gf_finiteType_affine_finite_cover_generated (seam 1),
%     lem:gf_affine_qcoh_Gamma_epi (seam 2),
%     lem:gf_qcoh_finite_sections_globally_generated (base case = seam 2 + 3).
% G1's proof then chains: cover-extraction (seam 1) + base case (Half 2) on each
% member + locality glue (Half 1).
% ============================================================================

\begin{lemma}[Finiteness is local on a finite spanning family]
  \label{lem:module_finite_of_localizationSpan}
  \lean{Module.Finite.of_localizationSpan_finite}
  \mathlibok
  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.LocalProperties.Submodule}).}
  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(N\) an \(R\)-module, and \(t \subseteq R\) a
  finite family with \(\mathrm{span}\,t = \top\) (the \(t\) generate the unit
  ideal). Suppose that for each \(g \in t\) the localization \(N_g\) is a finite
  module over the localization \(R_g\). Then \(N\) is a finite \(R\)-module.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[Half 1 helper — transfer of localized-module finiteness across models]
  \label{lem:finite_localizedModule_transfer}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.finite_localizedModule_of_isLocalizedModule}
  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(S \subseteq R\) a multiplicative submonoid,
  and \(M\) an \(R\)-module. Let \(R_S\) be an \(R\)-algebra that localizes \(R\)
  at \(S\), and let \(N\) be an \(R\)-module carrying a compatible \(R_S\)-module
  structure, together with an \(R\)-linear map \(\varphi : M \to N\) exhibiting
  \(N\) as the localization of \(M\) at \(S\) (\(\mathrm{IsLocalizedModule}\,S\,
  \varphi\)). If \(N\) is a finite \(R_S\)-module, then the canonical localized
  module \(\mathrm{LocalizedModule}\,S\,M\) is a finite module over the canonical
  localization \(\mathrm{Localization}\,S\).
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  The localization map \(\varphi\) provides an \(R\)-linear isomorphism
  \(e : N \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathrm{LocalizedModule}\,S\,M\) (universal property
  of localized modules), and the algebra localization provides an \(R\)-algebra
  isomorphism \(\psi : R_S \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathrm{Localization}\,S\). These
  are compatible: \(e\) is semilinear over \(\psi\), i.e.\ \(e(a \cdot x) =
  \psi(a) \cdot e(x)\) for \(a \in R_S\), \(x \in N\) (both sides are pinned by
  clearing a common denominator \(s \in S\), where the \(R\)-actions agree). A
  finite \(R_S\)-spanning set \(T\) of \(N\) therefore maps under \(e\) to a
  finite set whose \(\mathrm{Localization}\,S\)-span is all of
  \(\mathrm{LocalizedModule}\,S\,M\); hence the canonical model is finite over
  \(\mathrm{Localization}\,S\).
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}[Half 1 — G1 locality reduction over a finite basic-open cover]
  \label{lem:gf_finite_sections_of_basicOpen_finite_cover}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_finite_sections_of_basicOpen_finite_cover}
  \uses{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen, lem:finite_localizedModule_transfer,
        lem:module_finite_of_localizationSpan, lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}
  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB, proof, gluing step
  % (read from references/stacks-properties.tex, L2107-L2109).
  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
  % "Thus $M_{f_i}$ is finitely generated for each $i$. Hence we conclude by
  %  Algebra, Lemma \ref{algebra-lemma-cover}."
  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB (gluing step,
  Algebra Lemma \texttt{lemma-cover}).}
  Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(\F\) a quasi-coherent sheaf of modules on \(X\), and
  \(W \subseteq X\) an affine open. Suppose \(t \subseteq \Gamma(X, W)\) is a finite
  family with \(\mathrm{Ideal.span}\,t = \top\) (so the basic opens \(D(g)\),
  \(g \in t\), cover \(W\)), and that for each \(g \in t\) the section module
  \(\Gamma(\F, D(g))\) is finite over \(\Gamma(X, D(g))\). Then \(\Gamma(\F, W)\)
  is a finite \(\Gamma(X, W)\)-module.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen, lem:finite_localizedModule_transfer,
        lem:module_finite_of_localizationSpan, lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}
  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 01PB (gluing step).}
  By the local-on-a-spanning-family criterion \cref{lem:module_finite_of_localizationSpan}
  it suffices to show, for each \(g \in t\), that the localization of
  \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) at \(g\) is finite over the localization
  \((\Gamma(X, W))_g\). Fix \(g \in t\). The basic open \(D(g)\) is affine, and the
  restriction \(\Gamma(X, W) \to \Gamma(X, D(g))\) exhibits \(\Gamma(X, D(g))\) as
  the away-localization \((\Gamma(X, W))_g\)
  (\cref{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}); moreover the section restriction
  \(\Gamma(\F, W) \to \Gamma(\F, D(g))\) exhibits \(\Gamma(\F, D(g))\) as the
  localized module \((\mathrm{powers}\,g)^{-1}\,\Gamma(\F, W)\)
  (\cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}). Thus \(\Gamma(\F, D(g))\) is a
  model of the localized module, finite over \(\Gamma(X, D(g)) = (\Gamma(X, W))_g\)
  by hypothesis; the model-transfer lemma
  \cref{lem:finite_localizedModule_transfer} carries this finiteness to the
  canonical localized module of \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) at the powers of \(g\), over
  \((\Gamma(X, W))_g\). This supplies the per-element hypothesis, and
  \cref{lem:module_finite_of_localizationSpan} concludes that \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) is
  finite over \(\Gamma(X, W)\).
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}[Half 2, seam 1 — finite type yields a finite cover of globally-generated affines]
  \label{lem:gf_finiteType_affine_finite_cover_generated}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_finiteType_affine_finite_cover_generated}
  % LEAN STATUS: base-case seam, project-built (Mathlib-absent). Proposed decl
  % AlgebraicGeometry.gf_finiteType_affine_finite_cover_generated; extracts a
  % standard finite cover of W on whose members F is globally generated, from the
  % SheafOfModules.IsFiniteType local-generators datum refined by quasi-compactness
  % of the affine W.
  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB, proof
  % (read from references/stacks-properties.tex, L2101-L2107).
  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
  % "Assume $\widetilde M$ is a finite type $\mathcal{O}_X$-module. This means
  %  there exists an open covering of $X$ such that $\widetilde M$ restricted to
  %  the members of this covering is globally generated by finitely many sections.
  %  Thus there also exists a standard open covering
  %  $X = \bigcup_{i = 1, \ldots, n} D(f_i)$ such that $\widetilde M|_{D(f_i)}$
  %  is generated by finitely many sections."
  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB (standard
  cover of generated affines).}
  Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(\F\) a quasi-coherent sheaf of modules of finite type,
  and \(W \subseteq X\) an affine open. Then there is a finite family
  \(t \subseteq \Gamma(X, W)\) with \(\mathrm{Ideal.span}\,t = \top\) such that for
  each \(g \in t\) the basic open \(D(g)\) is affine and \(\F|_{D(g)}\) is globally
  generated by finitely many sections; equivalently, for each \(g \in t\) there is
  a finite index set \(I_g\) and an epimorphism of \(\OO_{D(g)}\)-modules
  \(\OO_{D(g)}^{\oplus I_g} \twoheadrightarrow \F|_{D(g)}\).
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 01PB (standard cover of generated affines).}
  Finite type of \(\F\) means that \(X\) has an open covering on each member of
  which \(\F\) is generated by finitely many sections. Intersecting this covering
  with the affine \(W\) and refining the resulting cover of \(W\) by standard basic
  opens, every point of \(W\) lies in a basic open \(D(g) \subseteq W\) on which
  \(\F\) is generated by finitely many sections. Since \(W\) is affine, hence
  quasi-compact, finitely many such basic opens \(D(g_1), \dots, D(g_n)\) already
  cover \(W\); collect their defining elements into the finite family
  \(t = \{g_1, \dots, g_n\} \subseteq \Gamma(X, W)\). That the \(D(g_i)\) cover
  \(W = \Spec \Gamma(X, W)\) is exactly the condition
  \(\mathrm{Ideal.span}\,t = \top\). A basic open of an affine is affine, and being
  generated by a finite family of sections is precisely the existence of an
  epimorphism from the corresponding finite free module. This is Stacks' passage
  from the local-generation datum to a standard finite cover of generated affines.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}[Half 2, seam 2 — $\Gamma$ on affine quasi-coherent modules sends epis to surjections]
  \label{lem:gf_affine_qcoh_Gamma_epi}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_affine_qcoh_Gamma_epi}
  \uses{lem:qcoh_affine_section_localization}
  % LEAN STATUS: base-case seam, project-built (Mathlib-absent). Proposed decl
  % AlgebraicGeometry.gf_affine_qcoh_Gamma_epi. This is the precise descent input
  % the base case relies on: across the gap1/gap2 affine qcoh ≃ Mod equivalence
  % (M ≅ Γ(M,V)~ over V = Spec B), the section functor Γ corresponds to the exact
  % forward functor, so a sheaf epimorphism descends to a surjective B-module map.
  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB, proof
  % (read from references/stacks-properties.tex, L2106-L2107) — the implication
  % "$\widetilde M|_{D(f_i)}$ generated by finitely many sections $\Rightarrow$
  % $M_{f_i}$ finitely generated" is exactly section-level surjectivity of a sheaf
  % epi over an affine.
  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
  % "such that $\widetilde M|_{D(f_i)}$ is generated by finitely many sections.
  %  Thus $M_{f_i}$ is finitely generated for each $i$."
  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB (sections of a
  generated sheaf over an affine).}
  Let \(V\) be an affine open of a scheme, with \(B = \Gamma(X, V)\) and
  \(V \cong \Spec B\), and let \(\pi : \mathcal{G} \to \F\) be an epimorphism of
  quasi-coherent sheaves of modules over \(V\). Then the induced map on sections
  \(\Gamma(\pi) : \Gamma(\mathcal{G}, V) \to \Gamma(\F, V)\) is a surjective
  \(B\)-module map.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:qcoh_affine_section_localization}
  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 01PB (sections of a generated sheaf over an
  affine).}
  Over the affine \(V\) the section functor identifies quasi-coherent
  \(\OO_V\)-modules with \(B\)-modules: each quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{M}\)
  corresponds to its section module \(\Gamma(\mathcal{M}, V)\) via the
  associated-sheaf equivalence \(\mathcal{M} \cong \widetilde{\Gamma(\mathcal{M}, V)}\),
  the affine instance (\(U = \top\)) of the qcoh-sections descent
  \cref{lem:qcoh_affine_section_localization}. Global sections of a quasi-coherent
  sheaf on an affine are exact (the affine has no higher quasi-coherent cohomology),
  so this equivalence carries the sheaf epimorphism \(\pi\) to a surjective
  \(B\)-linear map; that map is \(\Gamma(\pi)\). Hence \(\Gamma(\pi)\) is surjective.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}[Half 2 base case — a globally-generated finite-type qcoh sheaf has finite affine sections]
  \label{lem:gf_qcoh_finite_sections_globally_generated}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_qcoh_finite_sections_globally_generated}
  \uses{lem:gf_affine_qcoh_Gamma_epi, lem:module_finite_of_surjective_mathlib}
  % LEAN STATUS: base-case assembly, project-built (Mathlib-absent). Proposed decl
  % AlgebraicGeometry.gf_qcoh_finite_sections_globally_generated. This is seam 2
  % (Gamma-epi) followed by seam 3 (free surjection ⟹ finite). It feeds Half 1 with
  % each per-member Module.Finite Γ(X,D(g)) Γ(F,D(g)).
  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB
  % (read from references/stacks-properties.tex, L2094-L2107).
  % SOURCE QUOTE:
  % "Let $X = \Spec(R)$ be an affine scheme. The quasi-coherent sheaf of
  %  $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules $\widetilde M$ is a finite type $\mathcal{O}_X$-module
  %  if and only if $M$ is a finite $R$-module."
  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB.}
  Let \(V\) be an affine open of a scheme \(X\), write \(B = \Gamma(X, V)\), and let
  \(\F\) be a quasi-coherent sheaf of modules. Suppose \(\F\) is globally generated
  by finitely many sections on \(V\): there is a finite index set \(I\) and an
  epimorphism of \(\OO_V\)-modules \(\OO_V^{\oplus I} \twoheadrightarrow \F|_V\).
  Then \(\Gamma(\F, V)\) is a finite \(B\)-module.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:gf_affine_qcoh_Gamma_epi, lem:module_finite_of_surjective_mathlib}
  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 01PB.}
  Apply seam 2 (\cref{lem:gf_affine_qcoh_Gamma_epi}) to the epimorphism
  \(\OO_V^{\oplus I} \twoheadrightarrow \F|_V\) over the affine \(V\): the section
  functor yields a surjective \(B\)-module map
  \(\Gamma(\OO_V^{\oplus I}, V) \to \Gamma(\F, V)\). The source is the sections of a
  finite free \(\OO_V\)-module, so \(\Gamma(\OO_V^{\oplus I}, V) \cong B^{\oplus I}\)
  is a finite free \(B\)-module. A finite module surjecting onto \(\Gamma(\F, V)\)
  forces \(\Gamma(\F, V)\) to be finite, by
  \cref{lem:module_finite_of_surjective_mathlib}. Hence \(\Gamma(\F, V)\) is a finite
  \(B\)-module.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}[G1 — finite-type quasi-coherent sheaf has finite affine sections]
  \label{lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections}
  \uses{lem:gf_finite_sections_of_basicOpen_finite_cover,
        lem:gf_finiteType_affine_finite_cover_generated,
        lem:gf_qcoh_finite_sections_globally_generated}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}
  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (Tag 01PB, L2092-L2098;
  % the Stacks proof reduces finite-type on a standard open cover to the algebra
  % gluing lemma for finite generation of $M$ from finite generation of each $M_{f_i}$).
  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB.}
  Fix an affine open \(W \subseteq X\) and write \(R = \Gamma(X, W)\) and
  \(N = \Gamma(\F, W)\). Identifying \(W\) with \(\Spec R\), quasi-coherence of \(\F\)
  presents \(\F|_W\) as the associated sheaf \(\widetilde N\) of the \(R\)-module \(N\):
  on a basic open \(D(g) \subseteq W\) the sections are the localized module
  \(\Gamma(\F, D(g)) = N_g\) over \(\Gamma(X, D(g)) = R_g\)
  (\cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}). The finite-type hypothesis on
  \(\F\) says \(\F\) is locally generated by finitely many sections; restricted to the
  quasi-compact affine \(W\), this yields a standard open cover
  \(W = \bigcup_{i=1}^{n} D(g_i)\) on each member of which \(\widetilde N|_{D(g_i)}\) is
  generated by finitely many sections, i.e.\ each localization \(N_{g_i}\) is a finite
  \(R_{g_i}\)-module. The elements \(g_1, \dots, g_n\) generate the unit ideal of \(R\)
  (the \(D(g_i)\) cover \(\Spec R\)); by the standard finite-generation gluing
  criterion — finite generation of \(N\) over \(R\) is local on such a cover — the
  module \(N\) is finite over \(R\). Hence \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) is a finite
  \(\Gamma(X, W)\)-module.
\end{proof}

  \uses{lem:gf_finite_sections_of_basicOpen_finite_cover,
        lem:gf_finiteType_affine_finite_cover_generated,
        lem:gf_qcoh_finite_sections_globally_generated}
  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (Tag 01PB, L2092-L2098;
  % the Stacks proof reduces finite-type on a standard open cover to the algebra
  % gluing lemma for finite generation of $M$ from finite generation of each $M_{f_i}$).
  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB.}
  Fix an affine open \(W \subseteq X\) and write \(R = \Gamma(X, W)\). The proof is
  the chain cover-extraction \(\to\) base case \(\to\) locality glue.

  \emph{Step 1 — extract a standard cover of generated affines.} By the finite-type
  cover extraction \cref{lem:gf_finiteType_affine_finite_cover_generated}, there is a
  finite family \(t \subseteq R\) with \(\mathrm{Ideal.span}\,t = \top\) (so the
  \(D(g)\), \(g \in t\), cover \(W\)) such that each \(D(g)\) is affine and \(\F\) is
  globally generated by finitely many sections on \(D(g)\) — a finite epimorphism
  \(\OO_{D(g)}^{\oplus I_g} \twoheadrightarrow \F|_{D(g)}\).

  \emph{Step 2 — base case on each member.} Fix \(g \in t\). On the affine \(D(g)\),
  the globally-generated base case
  \cref{lem:gf_qcoh_finite_sections_globally_generated} (the section functor turns the
  finite epimorphism into a surjection of \(\Gamma(X, D(g))\)-modules from a finite
  free module) makes \(\Gamma(\F, D(g))\) a finite module over \(\Gamma(X, D(g))\).

  \emph{Step 3 — glue over the cover.} The hypotheses of the locality reduction
  \cref{lem:gf_finite_sections_of_basicOpen_finite_cover} are now met: \(\F\) is
  quasi-coherent, \(W\) is affine, \(t\) spans the unit ideal, and \(\Gamma(\F, D(g))\)
  is finite over \(\Gamma(X, D(g))\) for every \(g \in t\). That lemma concludes that
  \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) is a finite \(\Gamma(X, W)\)-module.
\end{proof}
